claim: Lost dogs can be classified into three behavioral temperament categories — Gregarious, Aloof, and Xenophobic — each predicting distinct travel distance, recoverability, and required capture tactics.
stance_target: temperament-based behavioral classification system for lost dogs
scope_conditions: Practitioner framework from Kat Albrecht/MAR, based on field experience since 1997; not yet validated by peer-reviewed study as of publication date
quoted_support: Wiggly-butt, friendly dogs are more inclined to go directly up to the first person who calls them... Dogs with aloof temperaments are wary of strangers and will initially avoid human contact... Dogs with xenophobic temperaments (due to genetics and/or puppyhood experiences) are more inclined to travel farther and are at a higher risk of being hit by cars.
numbers: Gregarious: found close to home or near escape point
Xenophobic: higher risk of vehicle strike
Aloof: often not recovered for weeks or months

claim: Xenophobic (fear-driven) dogs will flee even from their own owners, making direct pursuit counterproductive; they require baited traps or calm companion dogs for capture.
stance_target: never-chase protocol for fearful lost dogs
scope_conditions: Practitioner guidance from Albrecht/MAR; applicable to fear-spectrum dogs
quoted_support: Some of these panic-stricken dogs will even run from their own people! It may be necessary to use other dogs to get close enough to capture them or to use baited dog traps.

claim: The circumstances of disappearance determine initial travel trajectory: a dog escaping to explore a scent meanders and doubles back over short distances, while a dog bolting in panic from fireworks/thunder runs several miles in a straight blind run.
stance_target: escape-trigger as a predictor of initial search radius
scope_conditions: Albrecht practitioner framework; not quantitatively validated
quoted_support: A dog who digs out from a yard to explore a scent will tend to travel a short distance before she is found - meandering and doubling back as she explores the scent. On the other hand, a dog who bolts in panic because of fireworks or thunder will take off at a blind run and can run for several miles.

claim: The physical appearance of a lost dog significantly affects how quickly it is rescued by bystanders: small, purebred, or non-threatening-looking dogs are picked up faster; large, mixed-breed, or 'dangerous'-appearing dogs travel farther before intervention.
stance_target: breed/appearance as modulator of expected travel distance
scope_conditions: Albrecht practitioner observation; aligns with Albrecht 1999 informal data (mixed-breed vs. purebred distance gap)
quoted_support: People are more inclined to pick up small dogs, since they look vulnerable and are easier to transport and house than large dogs. In addition, people are more likely to attempt to rescue a purebred dog, whom they perceive to have value, rather than a mixed-breed dog.

claim: Population density is a major modulator of lost dog travel distance: high-density urban environments reduce travel before human interception, while rural/wilderness escapes allow much greater distances.
stance_target: population density as a spatial prior for search radius
scope_conditions: Albrecht practitioner framework; consistent with Lord 2007 and Weiss 2012 contextual findings
quoted_support: A dog who escapes in Manhattan will travel a shorter distance than a dog who escapes in the Rocky Mountains or in rural farmland. When dogs escape into areas with a high number of people, their chances of being found close to the escape point are increased.

claim: Terrain features — fences, cactus, heavy brush, steep cliffs — act as barriers that redirect lost dog travel along paths of least resistance.
stance_target: terrain as a constraint on lost dog movement probability distribution
quoted_support: Fences that create barriers will influence a dog's travel, since a dog will tend to take the path of least resistance when traveling. Cactus, heavy brush, and steep cliffs can be barriers that influence whether a dog continues on a path or changes directions.
